The article includes practical examples and code snippets to illustrate the functionality of Plan Mode, concluding with best practices for effective use.\n\n### Implementation Outline:\n1. **Setup**: Open Claude Code from your terminal.\n   ```bash\n   % claude\n   ```\n2. **Configuration**: Enable Plan Mode by pressing `Shift + Tab`.\n   ```\n   u plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)\n   ```\n3. **Development**: Use Plan Mode to research, analyze, and create a plan for changes.\n   ```\n   > reflect on your system prompt; hi can you explain plan mode\n   ```\n4. **Testing**: Review the proposed plan before execution to ensure it meets expectations.\n5. **Deployment**: Execute the approved plan using the exit_plan_mode tool.\n\n### Code Excerpts:\n```bash\n# Enable Plan Mode\n% claude\nu plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle)\n\n# Ask for an explanation of Plan Mode\n> reflect on your system prompt; hi can you explain plan mode\n\n# Example of planning a new UI specification\n> Lets plan specs/invent_new_ui_v4.md. By recognizing the fundamental differences between natural images and user interfaces, the method develops an innovative approach to token reduction. Utilizing the RGB color space, researchers construct a UI connected graph that identifies and groups visually redundant patches while preserving functionally critical elements like icons and text. The technique adaptively manages visual token complexity, demonstrating remarkable efficiency by reducing token sequences from 1296 to as few as 291 in sparse areas like Google search pages, while maintaining more granular representation in text-rich environments like Overleaf screenshots.", "metadata": {"title": "ShowUI: A Vision-Language-Action Model for GUI Visual Agents that Addresses Key Challenges in UI Visual and Action Modeling", "summary": "**TL;DR:**\n\n### Main Facts:\n1. **Technology Overview**: The article discusses ShowUI, a vision-language-action model designed for GUI automation, which enhances human-computer interaction by enabling intelligent agents to perform complex digital tasks.\n2. **Usefulness**: ShowUI improves efficiency in GUI navigation and task execution by bridging the gap between visual perception and computational processing, allowing for more human-like interactions with digital interfaces.\n3. **Implementation**: The article outlines the innovative techniques used in ShowUI, including UI-Guided Visual Token Selection and Interleaved Vision-Language-Action Streaming, which streamline the processing of high-resolution UI screenshots.\n4. **Code Examples**: While specific code snippets are not provided, the article describes the structured approach to managing actions in a standardized JSON format for diverse device interactions.\n5. **Best Practices**: Emphasizes the importance of meticulous data curation and augmentation to create high-quality instruction-following datasets for training GUI visual agents.\n\n### Abstract:\nThe article provides an in-depth exploration of Show", "web_address": "https://www.marktechpost.com/2024/12/01/showui-a-vision-language-action-model-for-gui-visual-agents-that-addresses-key-challenges-in-ui-visual-and-action-modeling/"}}]}

ARTICLEURL=https://medium.muz.li/ui-design-trends-in-2025-34987ed805c9 # UI Design Trends In 2025

![UI Design Trends In 2025](https://miro.medium.com/1*sOkarKseqH84zxnaXlFdOQ.png)

Designing for 2025 isn't just about hopping on the latest trends - it's about creating experiences that truly matters the most. As the digital world is changing faster than ever, UI design is shifting to meet new challenges and opportunities.

What's your take - will simplicity, bold visuals, immersive experiences or designs that truly connect with users define the best designs of this year?

So, without further ado, let's dive into the top UI design trends for this year!

## 1. Big Typography

Big Typography uses oversized, bold text as the main visual element.

Large, bold fonts dominate the composition, turning text into a key visual element. It grabs attention, enhances readability, and adds personality to the design, often paired with clean, minimal layouts.

ARTICLEURL=https://medium.muz.li/ui-design-trends-in-2025-34987ed805c9 # UI Design Trends In 2025

![UI Design Trends In 2025](https://miro.medium.com/1*sOkarKseqH84zxnaXlFdOQ.png)

Designing for 2025 isn't just about hopping on the latest trends - it's about creating experiences that truly matters the most. As the digital world is changing faster than ever, UI design is shifting to meet new challenges and opportunities.

What's your take - will simplicity, bold visuals, immersive experiences or designs that truly connect with users define the best designs of this year?

So, without further ado, let's dive into the top UI design trends for this year!

## 1. Big Typography

Big Typography uses oversized, bold text as the main visual element.

Large, bold fonts dominate the composition, turning text into a key visual element. It grabs attention, enhances readability, and adds personality to the design, often paired with clean, minimal layouts.

![[Heart Aerospace](https://heartaerospace.com/)](https://miro.medium.com/1

rBtkuhow3M0FAuZrBBjJ0A.gif)

![[Allta](https://www.allta.ie/)](https://miro.medium.com/1

EssA1b

L2VurCGMcqbecYw.png)

## 2. Brutalism

Brutalism features bold, large typography and a monochromatic color scheme, creating a raw feel.

The design avoids any unnecessary decoration and uses rigid, grid-like structure for clarity!

![[Northeast](https://www.northeastshop.com/)](https://miro.medium.com/1

frloEw_fKLZDz2w4okuhTQ.png)

![[Mouthwash](https://mouthwash.studio/)](https://miro.medium.com/0

yeo0uEfkED1byxws.jpg)

## 3. Morphism

Morphism blends sharp focus with smooth gentle blur transitions, creating a modern, fluid feel.

The fluid transitions between clarity and blur enhances the interface's depth and interaction, making it more intuitive and visually engaging.

![[https://x.com/tar_uniqueee](https://x.com/tar_uniqueee)](https://miro.medium.com/1

hcnMsGaMN3nVbaxoiwNZ1Q.gif)

hcnMsGaMN3nVbaxoiwNZ1Q.gif)

![[Augen](https://augen.pro/)](https://miro.medium.com/1

prjijiyGxFwR5Og_tcf

Jg.png)

## 4. Text Transitions

Text transitions add motion to text, making it more dynamic and engaging. They guide the user's eye, emphasize key content, and enhance usability.

Paired with other animations, they create polished, responsive designs that feel alive and intentional.

From subtle fades to bold effects, they bring typography to life!

![[Nathan Smith](https://www.nathansmith.design/)](https://miro.medium.com/1

2n1mTl9X5PuOdjkorKERSQ.gif)

![[Love & Money](https://loveandmoney.agency/)](https://miro.medium.com/1

No1ashxzfDowKbwBT6negA.gif)

## 5. Emoticons

Emoticon style combines text with emojis, making them a key part of communication.

It merges emojis with text seamlessly within paragraphs, adding personality and emotion to the design. It creates a more expressive, engaging way to interact while enhancing the overall interface!

![[Basedesign](https://www.basedesign.com/)](https://miro.medium.com/0

H4b2rf3_R2qrkjyt.gif)

![[Aaply](https://aaply.app/)](https://miro.medium.com/1

zwLuZTxHG6uN4tOfdRd_ng.png)

## 6. Lightning Dark

Lighting Dark design uses lighting effects, contrasts, and shadows to create depth and highlighting the important elements.

It makes dark interfaces feel more dynamic and visually dope!

![[Argus](https://argus

vpn.com/)](https://miro.medium.com/1

Hzq6NL5fH5qtNfvLO7dDrA.gif)

![[source](https://evervault.com/)](https://miro.medium.com/1

ZINCTOxQxg8E

1XnBr

YLw.gif)

## 7. 3D Elements

3D elements have moved beyond decoration to play an active role in design. They add depth, interactivity, and realism, creating more engaging and immersive experiences.

These elements guide users and enhance how they interact with content, making designs feel dynamic and modern.

![[Pierre](https://pierre.io/)](https://miro.medium.com/1

OicP6BjrSI0B7UWTA

cw2g.png)

![[Pierre](https://pierre.io/)](https://miro.medium.com/1

OicP6BjrSI0B7UWTA

cw2g.png)

![[Liquim](https://www.liqium.com/)](https://miro.medium.com/1

eCHbXMFDzSTOdRkLULI6GA.gif)

## 8. Bento Grid

Bento grids create clean, organized layouts that are responsive and flexible, ensuring designs look great on any device. They balance structure and aesthetics, making interfaces functional and visually appealing.

It's perfect for showcasing diverse content while maintaining clarity and hierarchy. This approach makes designs feel modern, engaging, and user-friendly.

![[Givingli](https://www.givingli.com/)](https://miro.medium.com/0

7j9NYkxox6Ychu2v.png)

![[https://dribbble.com/designopsagency](https://dribbble.com/designopsagency)](https://miro.medium.com/1

utUu9eqW3iVI9tesMtysUA.png)

## 9. Interactive Objects

Interactive objects brings life to design by responding to user actions with movement, animations, or changes in appearance.

They turn static elements into dynamic features, creating more engaging and immersive experience!

![[Loop](https://loop

music.webflow.io/)](https://miro.medium.com/1

8mrUN27nKrrzNlo6zwZLtQ.gif)

![[Artspace](https://dribbble.com/Synchronized)](https://miro.medium.com/1

I10vSvR4CzKhdoSaTft5aA.gif)

### Conclusion

As designers, we have the power to shape how people interact with the digital world, keeping up with trends is cool, but the real magic happens when we create something totally fresh.

And I've seen how simple design tweaks can turn users frown upside down. It's those little _aha!_ moments that make this job awesome!

So, let's get creative, stay curious, and always remember the best designs don't just follow trends - they set them!

@@ -1,1 +1,141 @@
- 
+ # PRD: Dynamic "Bento Grid" UI for Medical Dashboards
+ 
+ ## 1. Introduction
+ 
+ ### 1.1. Problem
+ The current UI generation is static, presenting information in a single-column vertical stack. This is not always the most effective way to visualize complex, interrelated medical data. Clinicians need a more dynamic and consolidated view to quickly assess patient status, identify trends, and take action.
+ 
+ ### 1.2. Goal
+ This document specifies a dynamic, grid-based UI generation system for the `UIDisplayAgent`. The agent will be capable of rendering medical data into multiple layouts—such as a single large view (1x1), a quadrant view (2x2), or a high-density dashboard (4x4). This approach, inspired by the "Bento Grid" design pattern, will allow for flexible and context-aware data presentation.
+ 
+ ### 1.3. Target Audience
+ This document is for the developers of the `UIDisplayAgent` within the `@/ag_ui_chat_project`.
+ 
+ ---
+ 
+ ## 2. Core Concept: The Medical Bento Grid
+ 
+ The "Medical Bento Grid" is a responsive and flexible layout system that organizes UI components into a grid. The `UIDisplayAgent` will be responsible for selecting the appropriate grid size and placing components based on the data's complexity and the clinical context.
+ 
+ ### 2.1. Grid Configurations
+ The agent must support the following primary configurations:
+ - **1x1 (Focus View):** A single component takes up the entire view. Ideal for focusing on one critical piece of information, like a detailed trend graph.
+ - **2x2 (Quadrant View):** The view is divided into four equal quadrants. Ideal for displaying a primary chart alongside supplementary information like alerts and recommended actions.
+ - **4x4 (Dashboard View):** A high-density grid for displaying a wide array of information simultaneously. Components can span multiple cells (e.g., a chart spanning 4x2 cells).
+ 
+ ### 2.2. Technical Implementation
+ - The grid will be implemented using **CSS Grid**.
+ - The `UIDisplayAgent` will generate the necessary HTML structure and CSS classes to define the grid and the placement of each component.
+ 
+ ---
+ 
+ ## 3. Component Definitions
+ 
+ The UI will be composed of several standard components. The agent should be able to generate these components from structured JSON data.
+ 
+ | Component Name | Description | Default Grid Size | Example JSON Keys |
+ | --- | --- | --- | --- |
+ | **Chart** | Displays time-series data, typically for lab values or vital signs. Includes a title, data points, and thresholds. | 2x2 or larger | `title`, `data`, `labels`, `normal_range`, `critical_threshold` |
+ | **Clinical Alerts** | A list of urgent or important findings that require attention. | 2x1 | `alerts` (list of strings) |
+ | **Actions** | A set of interactive buttons that trigger clinical actions (e.g., consultations, regimen changes). | 2x1 or 2x2 | `actions` (list of action names) |
+ | **Debug Info** | Displays technical metadata about the page load and data sources. | 4x1 (full width) | `debug` (object with key-value pairs) |
+ 
+ ---
+ 
+ ## 4. Example Layout Configurations
+ 
+ The `UIDisplayAgent` should intelligently arrange components based on the available data.
+ 
+ ### 4.1. 2x2 Quadrant Layout
+ 
+ This layout is ideal for a balanced view of a primary chart and related information.
+ 
+ ```mermaid
+ graph TD
+     subgraph 2x2 Grid
+         A[Chart Component (2x2)]
+     end
+ ```
+ *In a simple case, the chart takes the whole 2x2 space.*
+ 
+ A more complex 2x2 layout:
+ ```mermaid
+ graph TD
+     subgraph 2x2 Grid
+         direction LR
+         subgraph Top Row
+             A[Chart Component (2x1)]
+         end
+         subgraph Bottom Row
+             B[Clinical Alerts (1x1)] --> C[Recommended Actions (1x1)]
+         end
+     end
+ ```
+ 
+ ### 4.2. 4x4 Dashboard Layout
+ 
+ This layout provides the highest information density.
+ 
+ ```mermaid
+ graph TD
+     subgraph 4x4 Grid
+         direction TB
+         subgraph Row 1 & 2
+             A[Chart Component (4x2)]
+         end
+         subgraph Row 3
+             B[Clinical Alerts (2x1)]
+             C[Recommended Actions (2x1)]
+             B --> C
+         end
+         subgraph Row 4
+             D[Debug Info (4x1)]
+         end
+     end
+ ```
+ *Here, the chart spans the top half of the grid, with smaller components below.*
+ 
+ ---
+ 
+ ## 5. `UIDisplayAgent` Implementation Guide
+ 
+ The `UIDisplayAgent`'s `render_html` method must be updated to support this new grid system.
+ 
+ ### 5.1. Updated Logic Flow
+ 1.  **Analyze Input Data:** Receive the structured JSON from the `DataAgent`.
+ 2.  **Determine Layout:** Based on the quantity and type of data, select a layout (1x1, 2x2, or 4x4).
+     - If only chart data is present -> **1x1 Layout**.
+     - If chart, alerts, and actions are present -> **2x2 Layout**.
+     - If multiple charts or extensive data is present -> **4x4 Layout**.
+ 3.  **Generate Grid Container:** Create a main `<div>` with the appropriate CSS Grid class (e.g., `grid-container-2x2`).
+ 4.  **Generate & Place Components:** For each piece of data, generate the corresponding HTML component. Assign CSS classes to each component to define its position and span within the grid (e.g., `grid-item-chart-span-2x2`).
+ 5.  **Return Full HTML:** Output the complete HTML document with embedded CSS for the grid and components.
+ 
+ ### 5.2. Example Prompt Update for `UIDisplayAgent`
+ 
+ The prompt should be updated to request a grid-based layout explicitly.
+ 
+ **Updated Prompt Template:**
+ ```
+ You are a UI generation agent for a medical chat application. Your task is to create a dynamic "Bento Grid" layout using CSS Grid.
+ 
+ Based on the structured patient data below, select an appropriate grid layout (1x1, 2x2, or 4x4) to best display the information. Generate a single HTML file with embedded CSS for the grid container and its components. Components should be styled to be clear and visually appealing.
+ 
+ Data:
+ {structured_data}
+ 
+ Output (HTML with embedded CSS):
+ <!DOCTYPE html>
+ <html>
+ <head>
+ <style>
+   /* CSS for grid container and items */
+ </style>
+ </head>
+ <body>
+   <div class="grid-container-...">
+     <!-- Grid items go here -->
+   </div>
+ </body>
+ </html>
+ ``` 
```

</details>

---

I have created the document `ag_ui_chat_project/DYNAMIC_UI_GENERATION_PRD.md`. It defines the dynamic "Bento Grid" UI concept for the UI agent, including the different grid layouts (1x1, 2x2, 4x4), component types, and an implementation guide.

This document should provide a clear blueprint for updating your UI agent to generate the flexible, grid-based medical dashboards you described. Let me know if you would like any modifications or further details added.

**Clarifying the JSON Issue**

I've determined the issue with the JSON. It's the unescaped newline character within the string assigned to `"args"`. Specifically, it's the `\n` inside `"/home/gyasis/Documents/code/playwright-mcp/cli.js\n"`. This invalidates the string. I will explain this concisely.


**Diagnosing the Error Source**

I've determined that the issue arises from the unescaped newline within the "args" string. Specifically, the line break within `/home/gyasis/Documents/code/playwright-mcp/cli.js\n` is invalid. ---

Based on the snippet you provided, the JSON is invalid because of a **newline character** inside the string value for the `args` array.

Here's the problematic part:

```json
"/home/gyasis/Documents/code/playwright-mcp/cli.js
"
```

A JSON string cannot contain a literal, unescaped newline. The parser interprets that newline as an invalid character, which breaks the string and makes the entire JSON object invalid.

To fix it, you simply need to remove that newline:

**Corrected JSON:**

```json
{"command": "/home/gyasis/.nvm/versions/node/v22.9.0/bin/node", "args": ["/home/gyasis/Documents/code/playwright-mcp/cli.js"]}
```
